title: Oak serving board with through-tenon handle
subtitle: A solid oak serving board with a handle secured by a wedged through-tenon
excerpt: A thick-sawn oak serving board, 40 cm by 22 cm, with an oak handle secured by a through-tenon and wedge. The board is flat-sawn from a 40 mm oak blank and finished in food-grade oil. The through-tenon handle is the structural centrepiece: the tenon passes completely through the board and is locked with a single oak wedge.
sourceNotes: Victorian kitchen and farm manuals for chopping-board construction; Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for through-tenon joinery.

glossaryTerms.definition: A tenon that passes completely through the mortise piece and projects beyond the far face. The protruding end can be wedged, pegged, or left proud as a decorative feature.
A thin tapered piece of wood driven into a kerf cut in the end of a through-tenon to spread it against the mortise walls, locking the joint without glue.

content.text: Keep both hands behind the cutting edge. Clamp the workpiece firmly before chiselling or sawing; do not hold it free in the hand while cutting.

handle requires no screws and no glue. The oak-on-oak joint is strong enough for daily use and visible from both faces of the board; the proud tenon and wedge are the maker's mark. Oak is the right wood for a board that will receive water regularly: it is hard, close-grained, and does not absorb oils or taint food.

Seasoned oak: one board 400 mm by 220 mm by 40 mm for the serving surface. One handle blank 200 mm by 50 mm by 30 mm. One wedge blank 60 mm by 20 mm by 5 mm, tapering to 1 mm. Food-grade linseed or walnut oil for finishing.

Mark the mortise position on the board: centred on one short end, 20 mm from the top face, 40 mm wide and 25 mm deep. Bore out the waste with a brace and bit, then chop to the line with the mortise chisel and mallet. The mortise must be straight-sided and square to the board face; test with a combination square before cutting the tenon.

Shape the handle and cut the tenon at its board end with a tenon saw: 40 mm wide, 25 mm tall, and 50 mm long so that it projects 10 mm beyond the far face of the board. Mark the tenon shoulders with a marking gauge on all four faces; saw the cheeks first, then the shoulders. Test the fit dry before proceeding. The tenon should enter the mortise with firm hand pressure and emerge 10 mm on the far side.
With the tenon fitted in the mortise, mark and cut a kerf 15 mm deep into the centre of the projecting end. Drive the
into the kerf with a mallet. The wedge spreads the tenon against the mortise walls and locks the joint. Trim the projecting tenon and wedge flush or leave 3 mm proud as a feature.

Smooth all surfaces with a smoothing plane and card scraper. Apply three to four coats of food-grade oil, allowing each coat to soak in and wiping off the excess before the next. The board is ready to use when the surface no longer feels tacky.

items.symptom: Mortise walls split when the wedge is driven
items.cause: Kerf cut too close to the edge of the tenon, or wedge driven with too heavy a strike
items.fix: Centre the kerf carefully before sawing. Drive the wedge in stages: a few taps, then check, then continue. Stop when the joint is tight; forcing the wedge further risks splitting the tenon itself.
